title: Something is afoot in the land of Qwen
description: Simon Willison discusses Qwen 3.5, a remarkable family of open weight models from Alibaba's Qwen team. He notes concerns about potential high-profile departures from the Qwen team, suggesting this might be their swan song. The post highlights the rapidly evolving Chinese AI landscape and the importance of tracking international model developments.
author: Simon Willison

title: Anti-patterns: Don't inflict unreviewed code on collaborators
description: Simon Willison identifies a key anti-pattern in agentic engineering: filing pull requests with code you haven't reviewed yourself. This is described as 'common and deeply frustrating.' The guide emphasizes that developers must review agent-generated code before submitting it to collaborators, maintaining code quality standards.
author: Simon Willison

title: Donald Knuth on Claude Opus 4.6 solving open problem
description: Donald Knuth expressed surprise and delight that Claude Opus 4.6 solved an open problem he'd been working on for weeks. This represents a significant moment in AI adoption, as one of computer science's most respected figures acknowledges AI's problem-solving capabilities. Knuth celebrates both the solution and the 'dramatic advance in automatic deduction and creative problem solving.'
author: Donald Knuth (quoted by Simon Willison)

title: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite: Ultra-low-cost model
description: Google released Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite at $0.25/million input tokens and $1.5/million output tokens - 1/8th the price of Gemini 3.1 Pro. Simon Willison tested it with four different thinking levels, generating pelican illustrations at minimal, low, medium, and high settings. This continues the trend of increasingly affordable AI models.
author: Simon Willison

title: GIF optimization tool using WebAssembly and Gifsicle
description: Simon Willison built a GIF optimizer using Gifsicle compiled to WebAssembly, with a web interface for visually previewing and comparing different settings. The tool handles compression by identifying unchanged regions and storing differences, with options for palette reduction and lossy compression. Demonstrates agentic engineering patterns and Claude Code's capabilities.
author: Simon Willison

title: Anthropic's memory import feature is just a prompt
description: Simon Willison discovered that Anthropic's 'import your memories to Claude' feature is actually just a prompt that asks users to list all stored memories, context, and instructions. This reveals a pattern where AI features are often implemented through clever prompting rather than dedicated infrastructure.
author: Simon Willison

title: Claude Code v2.1.63: Multiple slash commands and memory improvements
description: Anthropic released Claude Code v2.1.63 with /simplify and /batch bundled slash commands, HTTP hooks for external integrations, and extensive memory leak fixes across the platform (20+ fixes). This update focuses on improving developer workflow integration and system stability.
author: Anthropic / Claude Code Team

title: Interactive explanations and cognitive debt
description: Simon Willison discusses 'cognitive debt' - when we lose track of how code written by agents works. While simple code doesn't matter, when core application logic becomes a black box, it becomes harder to plan new features and slows progress like technical debt. He advocates for interactive explanations that help maintain understanding of agent-generated code.
author: Simon Willison

title: An AI agent coding skeptic tries AI agent coding
description: Max Woolf's detailed account of becoming an AI agent coding believer after testing increasingly ambitious projects. He describes porting Python's scikit-learn to Rust as 'rustlearn' using agents. Woolf notes Opus 4.6/Codex 5.3 are 'an order of magnitude better than coding LLMs released just months before it' but acknowledges difficulty communicating this without sounding like hype.
author: Max Woolf (shared by Simon Willison)

title: Free Claude Max for open source maintainers
description: Anthropic is offering Claude Max 20x plan ($200/month) for free to open source maintainers for six months. Criteria: maintainers of public repos with 5,000+ GitHub stars or 1M+ monthly NPM downloads, with commits in the last 3 months. Anthropic notes they'll accept up to 10,000 contributors on a rolling basis.
author: Anthropic (shared by Simon Willison)

title: Unicode Explorer using binary search over HTTP range requests
description: Simon Willison built a Unicode Explorer that uses binary search over HTTP Range requests against a 76.6MB file. The demo visualizes each step of the binary search, showing fetch times and comparisons. He learned that Range request tricks aren't compatible with HTTP compression but CDNs like Cloudflare automatically skip compression with content-range headers.
author: Simon Willison

title: Continue v1.3.32: MCP App support and cn checks
description: Continue released v1.3.32 adding limited MCP App support, cn checks CLI command, enabled agent mode for LM Studio models, updated default model to Claude Opus 4.6, and consolidated React best practices. This release continues expanding MCP ecosystem integration.
author: Continue Dev Team

title: Andrej Karpathy on programming transformation in December
description: Andrej Karpathy shared that programming fundamentally changed in the last 2 months, specifically December: 'coding agents basically didn't work before December and basically work since.' He notes models now have 'significantly higher quality, long-term coherence and tenacity' and can 'power through large and long tasks,' making this 'extremely disruptive to the default programming workflow.'
author: Andrej Karpathy (quoted by Simon Willison)

title: Google API Keys security vulnerability
description: Critical security issue: Google Maps and Gemini share the same API keys, but Maps keys are designed to be public (embedded in websites) while Gemini keys can access private files and make billable requests. When Gemini API is enabled on a project with existing public Maps keys, those keys gain unexpected privileges. Truffle Security found 2,863 API keys in November 2025 Common Crawl that could access Gemini.
author: Truffle Security (shared by Simon Willison)

title: Cursor Bugbot Autofix with Cloud Agents
description: Cursor's Bugbot can automatically fix issues it finds in pull requests using cloud agents that run in isolated VMs with full development environments. Over 35% of autofix changes are merged into base PRs. Resolution rate increased from 52% to 76%. Community feedback highlights efficiency gains but also concerns about over-reliance and code understanding.
author: Cursor Team

title: Cursor Cloud Agents with Computer Use
description: Cursor released Cloud Agents with computer use capabilities - agents can now use the software they create to test changes and demo their work, producing merge-ready PRs with artifacts (videos, screenshots, and logs). Available on web, desktop, mobile, Slack, and GitHub. This represents a significant advancement in agentic capabilities.
author: Cursor Team

title: Claude Code v2.1.59: Auto-memory and /copy command
description: Claude Code v2.1.59 introduced auto-memory where Claude automatically saves useful context to auto-memory with /memory management, plus /copy command with interactive picker for code blocks. Community feedback was mixed: positive for solving cross-session memory pain points, but negative for performance issues and unwanted memory writing behavior.
author: Anthropic / Claude Code Team
